result.markdown:
--- title: "Phase 7 — Clinical Simulation" subtitle: "Typical patient, weight subgroups, and population variability (rxode2)" author: "Pramod BR" date: today format: html: theme: cosmo toc: true toc-depth: 3 code-fold: true number-sections: true fig-width: 8 fig-height: 6 execute: echo: true warning: false message: false --- ## Objectives With a model that passed diagnostics (Phase 6), we can now **simulate** — predict concentration-time profiles for scenarios we never measured. This is where a population PK model earns its keep clinically. We simulate three things with `rxode2`: a typical patient, an illustrative weight effect, and the full population spread. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} pars <- get_typical_params() dose <- rep_dose() ``` ::: The **selected model** is the **IIV model** from Phase 4 — Phase 5 found body weight was not statistically supported, so by parsimony the IIV model is the final model. Its typical parameters are CL ≈ 2.79 L/h, V ≈ 32 L, Ka ≈ 1.48 /h. Simulations use a representative **320 mg** dose (the median actual dose in the dataset). ## Typical patient Setting the random effects to zero gives the single population-average curve for a 70 kg subject. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} typ <- sim_typical(pars, dose = dose) sim_plot_typical(typ, dose) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Simulated typical-patient concentration-time profile.](07_Simulation_Report_files/figure-html/typical-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: Predicted **Cmax ≈ 8.37 mg/L** at **Tmax ≈ 2 h**, in line with the observed data and the NCA — the expected internal coherence of a sound model. ## Effect of body weight (illustrative) Because the selected model has no weight covariate (it wasn't supported), this is an **illustrative** exercise: we apply the **standard theoretical allometric exponents** (0.75 on CL, 1.0 on V) to the typical parameters to show the *expected* direction and size of a body-weight effect at a fixed dose. It is not an estimated effect. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} wts <- sim_weight_subgroups(pars, dose = dose) sim_plot_weight(wts, dose) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Illustrative typical profiles for 55, 70, and 85 kg subjects (theory-based allometry).](07_Simulation_Report_files/figure-html/weight-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} wts |> dplyr::group_by(WT) |> dplyr::summarise(Cmax = round(max(cp), 2), Tmax = round(time[which.max(cp)], 2), .groups = "drop") |> kbl(caption = "Illustrative Cmax / Tmax by body weight (fixed dose, allometric theory)", col.names = c("Weight (kg)", "Cmax (mg/L)", "Tmax (h)")) |> kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"), full_width = FALSE) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Illustrative Cmax / Tmax by body weight (fixed dose, allometric theory)
Weight (kg) Cmax (mg/L) Tmax (h)
55 10.57 2.0
70 8.37 2.0
85 6.93 2.1
````` ::: ::: ## Population variability Drawing 500 virtual subjects' random effects from the estimated ω matrix shows the **spread** of real-world exposures, not just the average. For a narrow-therapeutic-index drug like theophylline, this band is what matters — it indicates how many patients might stray outside the therapeutic window on a given dose. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} pop <- sim_population(pars, dose = dose) if (!is.null(pop)) { sim_plot_population(pop, dose) } else { cat("Population simulation requires an omega matrix (IIV model).") } ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Population simulation: median and 90% prediction interval (500 subjects).](07_Simulation_Report_files/figure-html/population-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ## Key takeaways - The qualified **IIV model** reproduces a sensible typical profile (Cmax ≈ 8.37 mg/L), confirming internal consistency with the data. - An **illustrative** allometric scaling shows the expected body-weight effect on exposure, clearly separated from the (unsupported) estimated covariate of Phase 5. - The **population prediction interval** quantifies between-subject variability — the critical output for dosing a narrow-therapeutic-index drug safely. - This completes the end-to-end workflow: data → NCA → structural model → IIV → covariate → diagnostics → simulation. > The full analysis is published as a Quarto website under `docs/` and deployed via > GitHub Pages. ```
